speaker: interviewer
text: --day would be at-- in Auschwitz for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we are-- you get up in the morning. And we were lucky we had water. We get water in that block 10. So we wash ourselves. This obviously was cold water we take, but water. And sometime we had a towel. We had to share it with two, three people. I was always together with my sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they bring you for breakfast?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we get only once a day food. But we had bread. We-- they bought us and-- and we get those shissels, you know. We get soup in it. And I get a slice of bread. I don't know if it was twice a day or once a day. I can't-- I think it was twice a day that we-- they get a piece of bread, I-- gave us-- to us. And we get a tea that-- in-- in the big-- Camilla tea or something like-- we get tea. And it was sweetened. And-- and it was only big piece. But you had to divide it yourself for the night, the day, you know. If people ate it, they had nothing in the night. They get one big piece of bread. You get one plate of soup. Normally, Lillian, my sister-in-law, she was very bold. If the soup was-- was-- I told-- I didn't tell you. She was mentally very strong-- a strong woman. And she-- she always tried to get for the second time soup. She went in the line if everybody has gone, she crawled on by. Sometimes she'd get hit. Sometimes they recognized her. Sometimes she said they saw her anyway. They still gave her a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ate to-- always-- we were always together. We ate together. We drink together. We-- we slept together. And I think it had a lot to do with staying alive. So you would get up in the morning--

speaker: interviewee
text: We get up in the morning, we stay--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you'd wash.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah, and we get up. We washed ourselves. We put our clothes on. I think we eat a piece of bread. We had a tea. And then sometimes we did it. We ate the bread already. We had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were staying until appell. At the appell it took at-- maybe at least 2 to 2 and a 1/2 hours. We had to stay, and stay, and stay, and stay, and stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in block 10, we were not allowed to get out. That was like a prison in a prison. We see the men. There was all men. And I told you it was in Auschwitz. It was not in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we saw the men going outside. Sometimes we saw-- we saw them staying a whole night in the cold outside without clothes, absolutely-- What were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just punished them for fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- oh, you saw the punishments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the punishment, you see them outside without clothes, or people laying on the-- I don't believe it how many dead people you see in a day. Everybody just dropped dead or get hit. People get hit. People get hurt. People--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do they do when someone drops dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came with a little wagon and picked it up. Just throw them in the wagon, take it away. Like when-- when we are-- were staying in-- in the-- in the-- that doesn't mean anything in Auschwitz. If you are in 1/2 a year, or 4 or 5 months in Auschwitz, that doesn't mean anything. You hope you die. I was always hoping I'd die fast, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lose your will to live? Or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my sister-in-law wanted to live. But I-- I really couldn't care less. I wouldn't be-- I-- I wouldn't be afraid to die. I was afraid to die how-- that he hurt me, that I was, you know, if there's-- in pain or annoying pain. But to die, just the dying wasn't mean-- they slapped-- you know. The people were so hungry. I see their mind go. Now, the only thing what they always used to say to the-- in block 10 people-- there was never enough food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you're skinnier, and skinnier, and skinnier. And there is not enough food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you spent your-- what did you do during the hours where you were not roll calling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, then after the-- yeah, we-- we-- this is also a whole different story. When I were a couple-- I mean, oh, couple of-- I-- I were a couple months in Auschwitz, in-- in the-- after three months there they call-- there comes the big trucks, and they take you away-- the people who are skinny, people who are sick, people who couldn't-- from the-- they get taked away. I was three times on a truck, and three times they took me off.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say I'm not finished with-- Mrs-- but you we never young. She-- we have to work-- I was-- other thing I have to tell. I was born with an-- a thyroid deficiency-- deficiency. I still get thyroid pills to the day, for today. But when I was six years old or seven year old, they find out I had a thyroid. And I get thyroid pill still every day now. I get every morning a thyroid pill.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can live with little food. I'm worried the fatter I get. I don't work it off. I don't burn it off. So I-- with all the people walking around a skeleton, I looked pretty good. I was never-- I never would have picked up that I was-- that they would say, oh, she is like a Muselmann, they called it-- so skinny. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Though they said-- they never would take me for it. Well, even whenever-- when my sister-in-law weighed at the end of the war 64 pounds, I weighed 125.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoa. Good storage system.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I-- when I came-- later, when I came out of the war and I went to-- by my doctors, they used to say, look what happened to the ladies-- went through the same thing, the same food. I asked-- you know, they never-- I looked good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I-- that was one of things that saved me. Then the other thing was that-- I told you Lillian was very good looking. She was blonde.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS always thought she was not Jewish. And they always used to say, you're not Jewish. You're not Jewish. And so an SS saw her walking with-- and he took her out of the row and said, you're not Jewish. And she said, yes, I am. And she was always with me. And she was afraid that she would be separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she asked-- she said, that is my sister. She used to say I don't want to go away. And she said that is-- then they said, but you don't look alike. You know, she said that is my father. And she looks like my mother or opposite. I don't know what they say. So she did not want to get a special treatment. Was-- she was afraid. So-- so we stayed always together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we one time, we were in a commando-- like we get out. And we went with-- had to pick her-- something out of the grass, and like little leaves and like-- like medicines. We went with two SS's and two dogs. And I had a big red stripe in the middle of my back-- red-- a red paint. And so-- and they went to-- they took us out, about-- it was about six people, and two dogs, and two SS's. And we were in the-- we went out of Auschwitz. And we were in a field. And sometimes one of the SS's let us pick apples, or they let us get some carrots.

speaker: interviewer
text: One time, we sat in a big field. And one of the SS's ask-- I mean, he said, are you hungry? I thought to myself, what am I going to say to him? I said I say to him, if you-- if you-- if I say yes, it's no good. And if I say no, it's no good. I said, why do you ask? You know what food we get. So he said, I have some bread in my knapsack. You can take it out. I think we saw the hairline. Take it out. If I take it out, the dogs are gonna go after me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, if you want to give it, you have to do it yourself. I'm not gonna get up and the dog gonna-- gonna go after me. But then he said, oh no. The dog wouldn't do anything if I don't tell him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he indeed he had bread with him. He gave every time that SS went with us. He gave us the bread, sometimes a piece of wurst, sometimes a piece of cheese. But after two months, he-- we didn't-- we didn't see him anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked of the-- are they-- squeal on him or he was placed on otherwise. But when that guy was here, he gave to us always food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was some kindness?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some. That's what I want to say. There was not. And he told us that he didn't know, that he came in Auschwitz what even in hell it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your sister-in-law didn't look Jewish. Do you know that there were any other people besides Jews there? Did you see any Jehovah's Witnesses or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I know there was not in our block. But when we were in Ravensbruck, I know there was people who are homosexual. I was-- it was people who were Jehovah's Witness. They were also-- and they were in the same camp as we are. They went through the same thing. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the block, was any-- were any of the women in this experimental block molested sexually?

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't molested. Sexually by the-- by the Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, one time they wanted to waken a poof they called it. They wanted to make an-- an-- an-- for the Germans. But then they came to the conclusion they-- there were a lot of Russian woman that they didn't need the Jewish women. So they made an-- an-- an-- an-- like an-- an-- how you say it? An-- an-- 'bordale'? A--

speaker: interviewer
text: A bordello?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for the Russian women. They were there. And they-- the-- the-- the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they make this bordello?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in block 10-- I think-- not in block 10-- in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not in our block. We were never--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took the Russian women for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for the Jewish women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any sexual activity between the inmates of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, what they were, yeah. There were. Like the blockalteste what we had, Margit, she was a-- she always lived with a woman. And there were more. There were more people who paired up. I know friends of mine. They were lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never know how they could do it. But that's what they did. I-- I don't-- I don't-- I don't think it's wrong. But I just-- I-- they just lived together. If they have-- they helped each other, so why not? It started at the camps and then they just continued?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they started at the camps. Maybe they were in-- in real life, but I don't know. I don't know. I know my friends, but I didn't know about their preference were. I-- I didn't-- I wasn't interested in it either. I don't care what they do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lot of times the men inmates would have to come and do work, I heard, to clean up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- they said it but I-- you see, there was three-- there was three floors in block 10. And I was in the third floor. And I really don't know what went down downstairs. The people said there was this there were that. I never. I know that Lillian had once in-- diphtheria. And she-- they went downstairs in the infirmary. They call it that. They have a little place. She didn't get extra food. She was very sick. And typhus she had, and her hair fell out. And I worked-- I-- I was-- I was already a designer when I went in Auschwitz. And somebody, the SS, was looking for clothes. She wanted a blouse made, or I don't know. And somebody told her, Elizabeth, she could. She very good sewer. She-- she worked for years. And I worked for her. And she gave me, always when I finished-- I-- I went to her, and I made her clothes. Like I'd get needles, and thread, and scissor, so I went-- and an iron. So I was working already a little bit. So I get some extra bread. You did everything for bread. For food, you-- you-- you did everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see stealing among people, from each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, stealing-- I thought-- in block 10, I can honestly say people did not steal from each other. They knew. What we did is also not nice, but if somebody was dead-- and already there was a lot of people dying all the time-- we were looking if they had food. We didn't say, sorry that she died. But we was looking if she had food. Maybe did sorry, or we felt sorry. But-- and we were talking to them when they nearly died, and they told us, you know, tell her, if you come back tell da, da, da. But we didn't-- for sure, we didn't come back. We were-- I was-- I, myself, personally, was absolutely sure I never would go out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any kind of religious observance being taken place by anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was like-- there was once with the-- they did. There were some Jewish people who were-- observed certain things, like with Pesach. They made-- we talked about, and there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that-- did you keep track of the calendar? Did anybody there keep track of the calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew that-- what times it was. We knew-- not exactly the calendar, but the people knew. It was also people came in with-- with-- with food downstairs-- the big-- the big vats with-- with-- with soup. Those men were-- were out of block 10. They came from the-- from the-- from the kitchen. So they used to-- they are more-- we were very isolated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- people, they told us the-- the war goes very well, and hang in there. And soon it's over. And try to keep your life. And you know, they really-- That was your connection with the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the connection, and at it is now winter. We could see outside winter or summer. And so that was things we-- that we-- we-- we-- we tried to-- at that time, at the end of 1944, it was getting bad already in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we tried to-- they had-- they used say, be brave. Hang in. You're gonna make it. You're gonna make it, you know. So that was slowly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the end came in-- in 1944, the end of 1944-- there happened a lot of things inside, but you know, the-- then the other little things. But then we-- the most thing we stayed alive-- like to stay alive. Like what you said, I sewed a little bit. Then I could-- I when I get needles, I give other people needles. They cut off just from the dress, they made a belt. You know, everybody could sew a little bit, especially Jewish people. We were mostly in the needle trade. And they-- they did knitting. We made knit-- we made-- knitted sweaters for the inmates. If they gave us wool, we made-- made sweaters for them. And we gave-- they gave us a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Though we never-- I never saw one-- maybe downstairs in the-- in the infirmary, and by the blockalteste, and the others ones lived. If the men could come in, they had to bribe them. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we never saw them. We were in the third floor. And we never know what went on downstairs. The only thing what we knew was when my sister-in-law was sick, and I get bread from when I was sewing. It was-- they sewing there. And I had to give bread for-- on her-- to give her something to eat, you know, a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I know there was a whole different department downstairs. They had-- they had more food than we did. So most probably they could find people who get in and out. That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't give it to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, But they traded something for it, probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most probably did. But I-- I was on third floor. We are glad that we-- we-- we stayed alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after block 10--

speaker: interviewee
text: So no. After block 10 it was already in the end of 1944. The Russians flew over. First of all, they blew up the-- the-- in-- the-- the gas chamber-- the Russian people-- Russians or Ukrainians, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: The crematoriums?

speaker: interviewee
text: The crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: The inmates did it

speaker: interviewee
text: They blow up the crematorium. They did it. They did. They sabotaged them. And we had to go all out. And we have to see them hang, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: The girls that did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had to see it. I closed my eyes anyway. But-- and there such a-- there such a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that day-- do you remember the chaos that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- yeah, we heard an-- an explosion. And people came all running out. They closed the-- all the blocks. And everybody had to get out. And like in the-- in the-- in the blocks-- but then, the next day, I heard they-- they made like-- where you hang out people, those-- how you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Gallows.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They hung out. And we-- we had to march all around it. And we-- they-- it's-- it's a mock. You know, it was like it-- that that day that they-- that they knew what they did. They sabotaged the-- the thing. And they do-- now they could die. And there was a prayer. And I-- it's absolutely stupid. Then we had to pass along with. I closed my eyes. I didn't want to see the hanging. And they were even say-- like something in Russian like long live Russia or long live the freedom, or, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that incident? Did anything change from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah, it was the same. When somebody-- it-- what was-- out to go out of Auschwitz, to fled Auschwitz, I don't know how you could do it. But it was all inserted with an-- an-- an-- an electrical thread. And if you want to go really, I saw people many times hanging in the threads-- 1/2 burnt-- dead, you know. There was such a high voltage on it. I saw people get punished. They stay between those two lines, and they-- they are incinerated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- I always say to Lillian, I want to die. But how? You know? I said. I not going to hang in the threads there. I'm afraid. You know? So what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then at the-- the end of the war came. The-- the-- those Russians who put papers down and said that we are-- we hang in, and this soon over. And we hear 'em flying. And we heard from everybody it was nearly over. The Russians were so close to Auschwitz. But- the SS-- the SS came, and they want us all out of Auschwitz. They did not want us to be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do have an idea what date that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The-- that was the end-- that was the end of December. It was the 1st of January-- two-- those two days-- the-- the 31st of December, the 1st of January. And it was bitterly cold in the [NON-ENGLISH], in Auschwitz. It was so cold. And I had no shoes. And I just was sick. I had diphtheria. And-- and oh, there's also a whole thing I have to tell you later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you want to know what happened to me? Now, I was-- I get diphtheria in-- don't you see, I still forget Dr. Slavkov. When I was in-- when I was in block 10, there was a doctor. She was gentile. And she was a prisoner, but she was not Jewish. And she was with those other doctors who did the experiments and things like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, every time she was there-- and her name is Dr. Slavkov-- and never asked us something else. Too bad I-- I don't know where she is. She does-- she helped me all the time. One time I get in a different blood group-- in my arm-- so that I got as if I have A something. They gave me a different blood group. And I was already gone. And it was very-- everything was spinning around me. And they wanted to see how long it took and how long it would take. And then, she let the needle fall out of my arm. And the SS was furious about her. And she did other things. When I had the needles, you know, that was like a-- looked like a burning, you know, like, I don't know what they put in it. And it was burning and bleeding. And you know, and then she came in the night and gave us water, and tried to wash us. And that was Dr. Slavkov. And she helped me many times. If she was getting a liking to us, I don't know, but she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later, when I was-- so now, we have to go back when I was in-- when we went out of Auschwitz. Everybody had to go out of Auschwitz. And I called it always the death march. When we went we came out. Death March.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Death March. When we-- you couldn't sit down. You couldn't go to the washroom. There was nothing there. And everybody who sit on the road, they get shot. So you were practically walking between dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they marching you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were marching me out of Auschwitz, through the snow. We were two-- two days marching, then we could sit down somewhere. It was snow. It was middle of the snow in the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- was it men, women? Who was in the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I was-- I was in-- in-- I was between men, and later it was women and men. We-- I was-- I was in block 10, and it was mostly men.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the whole plot, the whole of Auschwitz-- the hundreds, thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was being marched out all at one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone. If you were staying behind, they shot you. It was all the time shooting people. They didn't want to go, they were shot. There were some people who-- I don't know what they did. They were hiding. I know somebody who was in block 10 who was hidden. And an hour later, they were free. But I don't know. Maybe she was somewhere I don't know-- in a ditch. I don't know. We-- we were-- Lillian and me were not with that. We were in-- on the-- on the row-- on the walk. I didn't want to walk anymore. I wanted to sit down. And everybody was laying on the road half shot-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, God-- [SIGHS] death, and brains, and oh, unbelievable. That is something you can't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were walking and walking. And then, you know, and I wanted to sit down all the time. I was so tired. I had to go, you know, I said, I don't want to walk anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, they let us sit down in a field. It was all ice-- no food, no water. We had to eat ice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting in the-- on the-- on the road, and I-- somebody who was beside me was dead. I took the shoes. I had no shoes. My feet, all my toes, the nails fell off. And then, my-- my teeth were loose.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- anyway, we were walking and walking. Then we came to an-- I think it's past Gliwice. I don't know where it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was for days and days we were walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, we were in-- we-- we saw open train rails-- train cars. So we had to go in. And there was no roof on it. It was middle in January. Just-- so-- how cold. So you don't know where this was? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must be past Gliwice, but closer to Germany. They marched us. I don't know. We were in those-- in those open trains. Lillian climbed out of that-- of the thing. They were shooting at her. They didn't get her. And she stole a bread from the SS's. She had such a nerve, that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went on to the locomotive in-- of-- on the train. And she get hot our water, and it was warm. We had nothing to eat. And we shared it with other people with bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, when I came out of Auschwitz, when we had to go on the march, I had a coat. Before I went out, the blockalteste gave me coat before I went out. And it was a warm coat. And Lillian had a blanket. And we had this. So together, we-- we kept warm. With-- between the coat and the blanket, we tried to keep warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we were in that open trains, people just froze to death. There was nothing there-- no food, no heat, no washroom, nothing. And when somebody gets frozen, we just threw 'em out of the train. Well, you know, you lose your humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, threw out of the train. We get a little bit more place. It is unbelievable. So then, we came on-- in-- in Auschwitz-- in-- in Ravensbruck. Ravensbruck was a very small concentration camp. It was mostly for mischlings, they call it-- for people were 1/2, Jewish 1/2, gentile.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small-- a very small-- they had no place for all those people who came from Auschwitz, from Gliwice, from Birkenau. They had no place to sleep for those people. They took 'em, but they have nothing to do for them. They didn't know what to do with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came in-- in-- in Ravensbruck, Lillian weigh only 64 pounds. And she was just a skeleton. Her head, you know, and her-- and her bones, it was just a-- a breathing skeleton. So she couldn't walk anymore. We had-- I had to-- to-- to carry  her. But I told you I was always stronger than her. And they picked me all the time to-- to-- to go somewhere. I had to work. So when I came back-- Because you looked healthy--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came-- I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: --healthier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back and I now I had to only pray that she was still there. Now one time, she-- they-- they-- I came back. And she wasn't there. So they picked her up, the SS. And she was like in a Muselmann, you know, so, so skinny. And they took her to-- to the [NON-ENGLISH] to-- to-- to be gassed. So Dr. Slavkov was there. Lillian-- her mind was still good. And she was crying. She wants to go to me. She wants to go back. She wants to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Dr. Slavkov was looking for me. She came to the-- to the-- I was-- I was laying out. So I said get. You know what, maybe I have-- Elizabeth, she go, I have Lillian here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Said she cries. She cries. She cries. She-- she doesn't make it. But you take her. I rather have she-- she dies with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took her with me, you know, and-- and carry her back to where I was sitting. And then-- and a-- a day later-- so they took us all out out of that place and took us in blocks. They closed us up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The block was half the size. You see it 100 people. We couldn't lay down-- no drink, no washroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Ravensbruck. Neustadt-Glewe was the same. It is like, just beside it. It's the-- it's not in different. I think the whole complex is Ravensbruck. But at the-- they said Neustadt-Glewe, but it was actually, I think, Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in the-- in that place. And they closed us up. And you can imagine. People-- all the people were sick. All the people had diarrhea-- you know when you don't get food and you don't get water, no-- so everybody had [GERMAN], like diarrhea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was unbelievable. You are full with sores. We had lice climbing all over us. It was so sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Itchy, and burning, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unbelievable. So then, we-- the next day, the SS fled. They had all the uniforms outside. And then, the-- the people, in that-- in that little room, they heard the SS. We made a hole in the wall. We went around. And you opened up the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody what in-- in Auschwitz was-- I mean, there. The first thing, you look for food. Well, everybody was starving. You know, they don't get food. So they were looking for the arsenals if there was food in. And everybody was climbing over each other. And I said, what's going on there? I can't go near it. So I find some sugar on the floor. And when I was picking up the sugar-- and if I had a old coat on. I had to put the sugar in. And I want to go back to Lillian. And I heard somebody saying in Dutch, they said, are you Dutch people? I said, yes, I'm from Holland. He said, where are you coming from? I said, I'm born in Amsterdam. And he said to me, oh, do you look lousy.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, but I-- we have an-- an-- an cabin from the Germans, and we have a stove. And we have food. And we have water. I'm going to take you with other people, and take about six or eight people. And we gonna care for you. We are with four Dutch boys who want to help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wanted to get-- not go with them first, to Lillian. And Lillian was-- they came to Lillian. They didn't want to pick her up. She was so skeletal. So they get an-- an wheelbarrow, and they put her in. And they take her with. And the same night, we were laying-- and they-- and they took hot water, and they washed us. And who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Dutch boys. Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Dutch boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they-- they were in a work camp there. They were also picked up. But if you-- if you were able, they had to work for Germany. So those people were not SS's. They were just-- not in a-- in a-- in-- they called it a work camp. That was not like, like we, not in a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- then we were-- so then, the boys took us in. In the night, they leave for our bed. And we said, we can't, keep them. The little one, Lilli, is going to die for sure. And that the other one is also not far away. So we have to get them for-- by a doctor. Now the-- the-- the Russians started to-- to pester us also in the night. They came in with the-- with the guns. And one wanted to sleep with me. And the boy, they said they-- they couldn't Anyway, they said, it was-- it's much better when you go. You know, so-- not better because they took us-- they took us-- found-- they find a little rowboat. I don't know how. And they took us over the Elbe. And one side were the Russians at the Elbe, and the other the side of Elbe were the-- the Americans and the English people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they rowed you over?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they rowed us over there. And they had to go back where there was more people that they have to look after. So there we were on an-- on an-- on-- on an-- a-- on-- on-- on the end of the water. Lillian couldn't walk. And I had to find help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was looking around for soldiers. And then, first I saw one. They didn't understand we walked

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I remembered my number on my arm. So it was in about in April/May. So I had put my arm-- my arm up and I said, here. I said-- and then he-- and he was talking to the-- called right away people go, over here. I have somebody from the concentration camp. Come here. Come here. And then he asked me where I was. I said I was in Auschwitz. And, you know, and before I knew it, they picked me up. And they took me to the caserne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the caserne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the soldiers were. This is Dutch. The caserne is a Dutch word. And like-- like an-- how you call it? Where-- where the soldiers are?

speaker: interviewer
text: The station-- the army station.

speaker: interviewee
text: The station where they are. So they wanted to give me coffee. They want to give me chocolate. But I had to make them understand that I had to go back for Lillian. And I speak very poorly English. Not that I speak it so good now, but then-- you know, I said, you know, I have to go back. I have to go back. And slowly they started to understand me. And we went. And we looked for her. I forgot where it was. It took an hour. And I find her. And she was crying. And so was I. And then we-- and then, the guys also didn't want to pick her up-- went back for a-- for a stretcher and picked her up in a Jeep, and took us to the-- to their place.
